title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'Fashion and the planet: Write an article about fast fashion and its impact on the environment. What is the problem, and what can consumers do to make more responsible choices when it comes to clothing?'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
The True Cost of Fast Fashion

Every second, the equivalent of one rubbish truck of clothing is either landfilled or burned. This is the hidden reality behind the cheap, rapidly changing clothes that fill our wardrobes and the shops on our high streets.

Fast fashion — the model of producing large quantities of inexpensive clothing that quickly becomes unfashionable — is one of the world's most environmentally damaging industries. It consumes vast quantities of water and energy, generates enormous amounts of textile waste, and relies heavily on synthetic fabrics that shed microplastics into waterways with every wash.

The good news is that consumers have genuine power to demand change. Buying second-hand clothing from charity shops or online platforms such as Vinted dramatically reduces the demand for new production. Choosing to buy less overall, investing in better quality items that last longer, and supporting brands with credible ethical and environmental commitments are all meaningful steps.

Fashion need not come at the planet's expense. The most sustainable item of clothing, as the saying goes, is always the one you already own.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt:
Your school magazine has asked you to write a report on students' attitudes to fashion and clothing, based on a survey you conducted. Include findings about how important fashion is to students, how they typically shop for clothes, and recommendations for encouraging more sustainable clothing choices among young people.

Write your report.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Students' Attitudes to Fashion and Clothing at St Bridget's College

Introduction
This report summarises findings from a survey of 160 students aged 14–18, designed to assess their attitudes towards fashion and their clothing shopping habits.

Importance of Fashion to Students
Results indicate that fashion is a significant concern for the majority of respondents. Over 70% agreed that clothing plays an important role in how they present themselves, and many reported feeling social pressure to wear current trends. However, a growing minority — approximately 35% — expressed increasing concern about the environmental impact of the fashion industry.

Shopping Habits
The overwhelming majority of students reported buying new clothing from fast fashion retailers, attracted primarily by low prices and trend-led stock. Only 18% regularly purchased second-hand items, despite acknowledging that this would be a more sustainable choice.

Recommendations
The school should introduce a regular clothes swap event, enabling students to exchange unwanted items at no cost. Additionally, incorporating sustainable fashion into the design and technology curriculum would raise awareness effectively. Partnerships with local charity shops to offer student discounts could also help shift shopping habits in a positive direction.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You have read an article in a magazine arguing that school uniforms limit students' self-expression and should be abolished. Write a letter to the magazine editor responding to this article, explaining whether you agree or disagree with the author's argument and giving your own views on school uniform policy.

Write your letter.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I read with great interest the article arguing for the abolition of school uniforms. While the author raises some valid points about self-expression, I believe the case for retaining uniforms remains compelling and would like to offer an alternative perspective.

School uniform serves a purpose that goes beyond aesthetics. It removes the visible markers of economic inequality that can cause significant anxiety among young people when students are free to wear whatever they choose. In schools without uniforms, the pressure to wear expensive brands or fashionable clothing can be considerable, creating social divisions that directly undermine the inclusive environment that schools should foster.

Furthermore, research suggests that uniforms can help students adopt a more focused, professional mindset during the school day. The act of changing out of uniform when you arrive home creates a psychological boundary between work and leisure that many students find genuinely helpful.

Of course, uniforms should be comfortable, affordable, and updated to allow more flexibility — such as gender-neutral options. But abolishing them entirely seems unnecessarily drastic.

Yours faithfully,
A Secondary School Teacher
